content.assessment: This proposal operates through a fiscal-incentive mechanism entirely outside the structural-reorganization mechanism documented in the Polish case. The lens's core criteria (structural mechanism, distributional effects, reversibility, implementation sequence) were derived from evidence about tier reconfiguration, not funding conditionality, so the Polish precedent offers no direct evidentiary basis for evaluating this proposal's likely effect. What the Polish case does illustrate is that even direct structural levers took years to produce measurable, verifiable outcome data (2022 PISA data following a 2017 reform), suggesting that an indirect fiscal lever, one step further removed from the stratification mechanism, would likely require an even longer observation window before effects, if any, became measurable, and the current record contains no comparable case to validate this expectation.
opportunities: Could in principle be combined with structural reform as a complementary lever, though no Polish evidence substantiates such a combination
strengths: Reversibility is likely high in principle, since fiscal parameters can be adjusted without the physical infrastructure disruption Poland experienced (school closures, teacher displacement) [F-live-polish_reform-05]
threats: Given Poland's own experience that even structural reform effects took years to verify, a fiscal lever's effects, being more indirect, may remain unverifiable for even longer periods, risking symbolic rather than substantive change
weaknesses: No structural mechanism analogous to Polish tier reorganization is proposed, so the lens's primary explanatory framework does not directly apply
Lack of any comparable fiscal-conditionality case in the Polish evidence means the lens cannot assess likely distributional effects
